definition: R* estimation uncertainty spans ~480bp in combined 90% CIs across three leading models (Laubach-Williams, Holston-Laubach-Williams, Lubik-Matthes), exceeding the entire policy-relevant range of ~300-400bp. The Fed cannot statistically determine whether policy is restrictive, neutral, or accommodative within ±150bp, rendering r* near-useless for real-time calibration.
evidence: Three models produce point estimates ranging from 1.18% to 2.28%
Policy-relevant range is only ~300-400bp, narrower than the CI
Current cross-asset pricing internally consistent only at r* ~2.0-2.5%
Published model outputs directly verifiable from NY Fed, SF Fed, and Richmond Fed public data
open_questions: Can alternative identification strategies (narrative FOMC transcript analysis, event-study methodology) narrow the range?
Is r* even a useful construct for policy given the estimation uncertainty?
